pattern: Sorting the array with a prefix-sum table turns each query's total absolute distance into a single split-point lookup instead of an O(n) scan.
hints: The cost to make every element equal q is the sum of |nums[i] - q|; sorting nums lets you split that sum at the point where nums[i] crosses q.
Build a prefix-sum array over the sorted values so the sum of everything below or at any split point is a single lookup.
For each query q, binary search for its split point in the sorted array, then combine (q times count-below minus sum-below) with (sum-above minus q times count-above).

edge_inputs.description: a few queries against a small unsorted array
query far outside the array's range
query exactly equal to an existing element
query sits exactly between two clusters of values
many repeated queries with the same value
query smaller than every element in the array

stress.description: large arrays of nums and queries near the length ceiling
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 20000))
    m = max(1, min(size, 20000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    queries = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(m)]
    return [nums, queries]

solutions.python:
import bisect
class Solution:
    def minOperations(self, nums, queries):
        s = sorted(nums)
        n = len(s)
        prefix = [0] * (n + 1)
        for i, v in enumerate(s):
            prefix[i + 1] = prefix[i] + v
        total = prefix[n]
        res = []
        for q in queries:
            idx = bisect.bisect_right(s, q)
            left_sum = prefix[idx]
            left_count = idx
            right_sum = total - left_sum
            right_count = n - idx
            cost = (q * left_count - left_sum) + (right_sum - q * right_count)
            res.append(cost)
        return res
